source.title: Loss of Containment, Fires, and Explosions at Enterprise Products Midstream Gas Plant

event.costs: $10.4 million fire response expense; $7.1 million non-cash asset loss
actions_taken: Board operator initiated ESD 1000 approximately one minute after initial explosion
Board operator initiated ESD 9900 twenty-three seconds after ESD 1000
Plant operations supervisor called 9-1-1 after being notified
Emergency responders allowed fire to burn itself out while cooling equipment with water monitors
Pascagoula Fire Department set up checkpoint at nearest major intersection
Jackson County Emergency Services established unified command
Board operator reset 9900 system at 11:33 p.m. after observing vent fire on B-Train demethanizer, then reinitiated at 11:45 p.m.
Site shut down for almost six months
event.summary: On June 27, 2016, a major loss of containment occurred at the Enterprise Products Pascagoula Gas Plant (PGP) in Pascagoula, Mississippi. The probable cause was the failure of a brazed aluminum heat exchanger (BAHX)—specifically the A-Train cold side reboiler (ACSR)—due to accumulated thermal fatigue. Blocked layers from a 2015 repair, with vent holes subsequently welded closed, became over-pressurized when process fluids leaked into them through thermal fatigue cracks and were heated during a curtailment shutdown procedure. The resulting catastrophic rupture released methane, ethane, propane, and other hydrocarbons, which ignited, causing a series of fires and explosions. Thirteen separate equipment ruptures occurred over approximately 35 minutes. Two workers on the night shift were uninjured. The site was shut down for nearly six months. Enterprise incurred $10.4 million in fire response costs and a $7.1 million non-cash asset loss.
recommendations: API (2016-02-I-MS-R1): Develop or incorporate into API 668 guidance on safe operation, maintenance, and repair of BAHXs addressing thermal fatigue hazards, sensor placement, and venting of blocked layers.
GPA Midstream (2016-02-I-MS-R2): Revise GPA Technical Bulletin on BAHXs to incorporate lessons learned including catastrophic failure potential, sensor placement, and venting of blocked layers.
GPA Midstream (2016-02-I-MS-R3): Develop a database for operators to submit BAHX operational data for collaborative industry learning and analysis.
GPA Midstream (2016-02-I-MS-R4): Using collected BAHX operational data, continue analysis to determine correlation between process data and thermal fatigue cracking frequency to predict service life.
Jackson County LEPC (2016-02-I-MS-R5): Define and publish community notification methods and expectations including social media, local news, and passive phone systems.

hazards.name: Flammable hydrocarbons under pressure in cryogenic BAHX
Accumulated thermal fatigue damage in brazed aluminum heat exchanger

threats.name: Thermal fatigue cracking of BAHX core causing interpass leaks into blocked layers
Over-pressurization of blocked/unvented BAHX layers due to trapped hydrocarbon thermal expansion
Erratic two-phase flow and compressor shutdowns causing excessive temperature rate-of-change exceedances
Inadequate repair: vent holes welded closed after blocking layers, removing pressure relief path

consequences.name: Catastrophic BAHX rupture and large-scale hydrocarbon release
Fires and multiple explosions across A-Train process area
Six-month plant shutdown and significant financial loss ($17.5 million combined)
Community alarm, self-evacuation, and inadequate public notification
Potential worker fatalities (avoided due to night shift low staffing)

controls.name: BAHX temperature rate-of-change (ROC) operating procedures and limits
controls.barrier_role: Procedural control to limit thermal stress on BAHX by restricting heating/cooling rates during start-up, shutdown, and normal operation
controls.lod_basis: Primary operational control relied upon by PGP to manage thermal fatigue risk

supporting_text: PGP made several improvements to optimize its gas processing operation and manage both the internal and external challenges. The improvements used administrative controls, such as procedural changes.
This time, the procedure modifications cited industry guidance to control the rate of temperature change on the exchanger core to less than 3.5°F/min (2°C/min).
process data from the ACSR shows that both companies struggled to operate the BAHXs within the confines of the RAGAGEP guidance operational parameters during all phases of operations

controls.name: Metal temperature sensors with ROC alarms on BAHX
controls.barrier_role: Engineering monitoring to detect excessive temperature rates of change on BAHX metal surfaces and alert operators
controls.lod_basis: Secondary detection layer installed after 2010 root cause investigation

supporting_text: PGP alarmed the metal temperature sensors, but not the stream temperature sensors, to alert operators to temperature problems in the exchangers.

if gas plant personnel monitor for thermal stresses using metal temperatures, then they could be misled to believe that their responses to the operational conditions are sufficient in minimizing thermal fatigue damage
ROCs calculated from the metal temperatures are about 10 times less than those for the stream temperatures.
at the time of the incident, the CSB determined that the set point for the metal temperature sensor alarms was actually 14.25°F/min

controls.name: Periodic BAHX leak detection and repair program (leak-before-break approach)
controls.barrier_role: Mechanical integrity program relying on detection of small leaks before catastrophic failure, followed by field repair
controls.lod_basis: Primary mechanical integrity strategy at PGP for BAHX management

supporting_text: PGP's approach to the mechanical integrity of its BAHXs was leak detection and repair.
the reliance on a leak-before-failure assumption is not sufficient
the June 2016 PGP incident, as well as four other BAHX failure events, illustrate that the reliance on a leak-before-failure assumption is not sufficient
PGP did not conduct any calculations to determine the critical size of a crack required to result in a catastrophic failure of the exchanger

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) review for BAHX layer blocking repair
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to assess hazards introduced by blocking BAHX layers and ensure safe repair procedures
controls.lod_basis: PSM-required MOC process for equipment modifications

supporting_text: Neither BP nor Enterprise provided the 'what-if' hazard review with the MOC documentation, so the CSB could not determine which engineering standards BP assessed, how it assessed them, or whether it considered the over-pressurization of the blocked passes a potential hazard.
The MOC documentation also indicates that the blocking of passes was in accordance with BP engineering and industry standards.
OSHA fined PGP for not ensuring that the site retained documentation associated with all process hazard analyses, updates, and revalidations

controls.name: Vent holes in blocked BAHX layers to prevent over-pressurization
controls.barrier_role: Engineering safeguard to relieve pressure from blocked layers if process fluids leak into them
controls.lod_basis: Required by API and ALPEMA industry standards; critical safeguard against blocked-layer over-pressurization

supporting_text: the vent holes for the blocked layers were welded closed
Without open vent holes, the blocked layers had no means to relieve pressure should hydrocarbons leak into one of them through a thermal fatigue–induced crack.

Industry guidance by both the American Petroleum Institute (API) and the Aluminium Plate-Fin Heat Exchanger Manufacturer's Association (ALPEMA) indicates that inactive areas, including blocked and/or dummy layers at the top and bottom of exchangers, shall be vented
Post-incident examination confirmed that vent holes had been welded closed.

controls.name: Post-repair leak test of blocked BAHX layers (pressurization test)
controls.barrier_role: Quality assurance check to confirm no interpass cracks exist between blocked and active layers after repair
controls.lod_basis: Stipulated in 2015 repair procedures but not confirmed as completed

supporting_text: No records show that workers completed this test to confirm that interpass cracks did not exist between layers 98 and 97.
No documentation confirms that this test was conducted; therefore, the possibility of an interpass leak existing between layers 98 and 97 at the time of the 2015 repair cannot be ruled out.
no documentation confirms that any leak test was performed after the layers were blocked

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) and revalidation
controls.barrier_role: Systematic hazard identification and risk assessment to identify BAHX thermal fatigue risks and recommend controls
controls.lod_basis: PSM-required element; conducted at plant inception and revalidated every five years

supporting_text: The 2013 PHA revalidation no longer identified health and safety consequences associated with the exchangers. Instead, the listed potential consequences mirrored the plant's experiences with leaks.
The potential for severe injuries, death, or significant financial losses was not identified.
over the years between the first documented BAHX hydrocarbon leak and the June 2016 loss of containment (LOC), PGP risk perception focused on minor leaks and financial consequences of repair.
OSHA fined PGP for not ensuring that the site retained documentation associated with all process hazard analyses

controls.name: Emergency Shut Down (ESD) systems ESD 1000 and ESD 9900
controls.barrier_role: Automated and manually initiated systems to block flows, depressurize plant, and route hydrocarbons to flare to limit escalation after loss of containment
controls.lod_basis: First line of mitigation after LOC event; activated within one minute of explosion

supporting_text: The board operator initiated ESD 1000 about a minute after the initial explosion, and then, after conferring with the outside operator about the severity of the event, hit the ESD 9900 button—23 seconds after initiating ESD 1000.

ESD 1000 blocks all the gas and liquid flows into and out of the plant and shuts down some equipment, but does not depressurize the plant. ESD 9900 goes a step further and vents the gas in a predetermined sequence by sending most of it to the flares

controls.name: Emergency response plan and 'let it burn' fire strategy
controls.barrier_role: Pre-planned emergency response strategy to allow contained fire to burn out, preventing vapor cloud explosion beyond property boundary
controls.lod_basis: Established emergency response philosophy designed to minimize off-site vapor cloud explosion risk

supporting_text: PGP's emergency response plan was to vent fuel to the flare or vent to atmosphere and to let any existing fire burn until it self-extinguished.
The 'let it burn' philosophy was established as a means to minimize the likelihood of a vapor cloud explosion that could extend outside the property line.
Emergency responders, in conjunction with PGP management personnel, decided the appropriate course of action would be to allow the fire to burn itself out, while cooling equipment where feasible.

controls.name: Blast-resistant control room module
controls.barrier_role: Engineering protection for personnel in control room from blast and fire effects
controls.lod_basis: Physical protection installed in 2010/2012 to protect operations personnel

supporting_text: Blast-resistant modules (BRMs) were installed onsite in 2010 to move administrative and engineering personnel away from areas that could be affected by potential fires and explosions. In 2012, operations personnel and the control room were also moved into a BRM.
Within a minute of the initial explosion, the operators activated the emergency shut down systems at the plant and sheltered in the control room.

controls.name: Flare system for controlled hydrocarbon disposal
controls.barrier_role: Engineering system to safely burn off released hydrocarbons and reduce uncontrolled vapor cloud formation
controls.lod_basis: Integral part of emergency depressurization and fire mitigation strategy

supporting_text: Plants like PGP use flares to dispose of flammable gas in the event of an emergency by burning it in a safe and controlled manner.
emergency systems depressurized the plant and sent much of the process fluids to a flare
Enterprise reported that 104,000 pounds of total emissions were released on June 27, 2016; most of the released hydrocarbon was burned off by the ensuing fires and flares.

controls.name: Community notification and emergency public communication
controls.barrier_role: Administrative system to inform and protect the public during chemical emergency events
controls.lod_basis: Required under EPA RMP rule; relied upon social media and local media rather than reverse 9-1-1

supporting_text: Emergency responders also determined that the residential population and property would not be physically harmed by the event, and thus did not initiate a reverse 9-1-1 call, relying instead upon the local media outlets and social media to keep the public informed.
members of a local community organization expressed concern to the CSB that some residents did not know how to respond. They felt uninformed and ill equipped to know if they were in harm's way.
we found out more through Facebook than we did from anything [else]

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.

pif_ids_referenced.PIF-001: Operator procedures for curtailment/shutdown - procedural knowledge
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-002: Operator situational awareness - no abnormal alarms before explosion
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-003: Misleading metal temperature sensor data vs stream temperature data
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-004: Inadequate repair documentation and quality assurance by HETSCO/BP
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-005: Organisational risk perception drift - PHA underestimated catastrophic failure potential
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-006: Safety culture - acceptance of leaks as normal, failure to escalate to replacement
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-007: Operator competence in activating ESD systems promptly after explosion
